text: All right, welcome to another episode. Today we're talking about robotic inguinal hernia repair in patients who have previously had preperitoneal dissection, specifically with a tap inguinal hernia on the contralateral side. This was a seventy-two-year-old man presented to my clinic with multiple hernia procedures, a symptomatic recurrent left inguinal hernia, surgical history, bilateral open inguinal hernia repairs that were tissue-based, And then a right contralateral TEP inguinal hernia repair with mesh. They, during the operation, explored the left side with no evidence of hernia. He had irreducible left inguinal hernia. Again, no mesh on that side.

But we did review their prior surgical history and operator reports found that they had used a balloon dissector during the prior repair. No significant dissection really described, acknowledged that the open repairs had probably caused some complex anatomy for an open anterior repair. And so we planned our operation. Patient previously had an umbilical hernia repair with mesh, so we were going to definitely avoid umbilical trocar. I booked an extra thirty minutes for the case to account for potential need for license of adhesions and need for a more careful dissection of the preperitoneal plane. For this, I thought a robotic tab was still a very safe option.

The surgeon begins the procedure by developing the preperitoneal flap. An indirect hernia is visible on the left side with no mesh present, while the silhouette of a mesh is observed on the contralateral side, not extending beyond the midline. The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue using heat to minimize bleeding, avoiding blunt dissection due to potential adhesions.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection, unaffected by previous operations, followed by a medial dissection. The medial aspect of the contralateral mesh is visible, positioned away from the dissection area. The contralateral pubis is exposed to ensure a proper landing zone for the mesh. The surgeon then focuses on reducing the hernia sac, carefully dissecting surrounding tissue to facilitate the reduction.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue to identify the beginning of the hernia sac and spermatic structures, revealing a well-demarcated edge of the sac. The dissection continues to establish a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, parietalizing the vas and vasculature. The vas is visible, and the surgeon emphasizes creating a landing zone for the mesh. The previously dissected space shows mesh on top of the bladder, with a clear space for mesh placement. The surgeon removes extra tissue from the femoral canal, noting the absence of a femoral hernia, and highlights the external iliac artery entering the femoral canal while developing the space.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection, revealing the iliopubic tract. Using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, the lateral space is developed. The surgeon carefully manipulates the spermatic cord structures to tease the peritoneum down, avoiding excessive tissue handling. The pre-peritoneal aspect of the medial umbilical ligament is dissected to allow the vas to retract over the external iliac vein, achieving a clear view of the myopectenial orifice.

The surgeon points out anatomical landmarks, drawing an imaginary line from Cooper's ligament to the lateral sidewall. A mid-weight, extra-large mesh is positioned and fixated with a single stitch over Cooper's ligament. The corner of the mesh is visible, slightly protruding, but is addressed off-camera.

The surgeon adjusts the corners of the mesh to ensure proper positioning, preventing clam shelling that could lead to recurrence. A standard flap closure is performed, addressing a visible hole below. The robotic inguinal hernia surgery allows for refined sewing techniques, minimizing the consequences of peritoneal holes.
